--- SOURCE 1: The Importance of Vaccinations in Preventing Infectious Diseases ---
URL: https://www.austinid.net/blog/1290912-the-importance-of-vaccinations-in-preventing-infectious-diseases/

SUMMARY:
Vaccinations are a cornerstone of public health, designed to protect individuals and communities from the spread of infectious diseases.

FULL CONTENT:
Austin Infectious Disease Consultants

# The Importance of Vaccinations in Preventing Infectious Diseases

![Medical-doctor-preparing-a-syringe-for-patient](https://smb.ibsrv.net/imageresizer/image/article_manager/1200x1200/229165/1290912/heroimage0.677557001741278358.jpg "Vaccine-Being-Prepared-by-the-Doctor")

At Austin Infectious Disease Consultants, located in Austin, TX, we are dedicated to protecting the health of our community by preventing and treating infectious diseases. One of the most effective tools in combating these diseases is vaccination. Our experienced team provides comprehensive vaccination services to help individuals and families stay protected against a wide range of illnesses.

## **How Vaccinations Prevent Infectious Diseases**

Vaccinations are a cornerstone of public health, designed to protect individuals and communities from the spread of infectious diseases. Vaccines work by stimulating the immune system to recognize and fight specific pathogens, such as viruses or bacteria, without causing the illness itself. This process not only protects the vaccinated individual but also contributes to herd immunity, reducing the overall spread of disease and protecting those who cannot be vaccinated due to medical reasons. Diseases such as measles, polio, and influenza, which once caused widespread illness and death, have been significantly reduced or even eradicated in some regions thanks to vaccination efforts. However, the continued emergence of new infectious diseases, such as COVID-19, highlights the ongoing importance of staying up to date on recommended vaccines.

## **Common Vaccines and Their Role in Disease Prevention**

Vaccines are available to protect against a wide range of infectious diseases. Routine immunizations, such as those for measles, mumps, rubella (MMR), diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (DTaP), are typically administered during childhood to provide early protection. Influenza vaccines are recommended annually to protect against seasonal flu, which can cause severe complications in vulnerable populations.

For adults, vaccines such as the shingles vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine, and hepatitis vaccines are important for preventing diseases that can have serious health consequences. Travel vaccines, such as those for yellow fever or typhoid, are essential for individuals visiting regions where certain infectious diseases are more prevalent. At Austin Infectious Disease Consultants, we provide a full range of vaccinations tailored to your age, health status, and lifestyle. Our team ensures that you receive the right vaccines at the right time to keep you protected.

## **The Risks of Skipping Vaccinations**

Choosing not to vaccinate can leave individuals vulnerable to serious and potentially life-threatening infectious diseases. It also increases the risk of outbreaks within the community, particularly among those who cannot be vaccinated due to medical conditions. Vaccines are rigorously tested for safety and effectiveness, and the benefits of vaccination far outweigh the risks of rare side effects.

By staying up to date on vaccinations, you not only protect yourself but also contribute to the health and safety of your family, friends, and community. Vaccination is a simple yet powerful way to prevent the spread of infectious diseases and ensure a healthier future for everyone.

## **Schedule Your Vaccination Appointment Today**

If youâ€™re due for vaccinations or want to learn more about how to protect yourself from infectious diseases, we invite you to visit Austin Infectious Disease Consultants. For more information about our services, please visit our website at [Austin Infectious Disease Consultants](https://www.austinid.net/) or call us at [(512)-459-0301](tel:5124590301).

![Medical-doctor-preparing-a-syringe-for-patient](https://smb.ibsrv.net/imageresizer/image/article_manager/1200x1200/229165/1290912/heroimage0.677557001741278358.jpg "Vaccine-Being-Prepared-by-the-Doctor")

--- SOURCE 4: How vaccines work to protect us - OSF HealthCare ---
URL: https://www.osfhealthcare.org/blog/how-vaccines-work-to-protect-us

SUMMARY:
Vaccinations prevent diseases that can be dangerous or even deadly. They reduce the risk of infection by working with the body's natural defenses to develop

FULL CONTENT:
# How vaccines work to protect us

Are your vaccines up-to-date?

[> Ask your provider](https://providers.osfhealthcare.org/)

Vaccinations prevent diseases that can be dangerous or even deadly. They reduce the risk of infection by working with the bodyâ€™s natural defenses to develop immunity safely.

[Vaccinations](http://healthlibrary.osfhealthcare.org/Search/40,ImmunQuiz) can benefit both the people who get them and vulnerable, unvaccinated people around them. Thatâ€™s because the infection has fewer people it can spread to through the community if most people are immunized.

## How our bodies fight infection

There is much uncertainty, even fear, surrounding vaccinations. Most of the apprehension about vaccines comes from a lack of knowledge or understanding of how vaccinations help the body protect itself.

â€œOur bodies are made up of trillions of cells, each with a purpose. While still working on the total, scientists estimate the number of cells at around 30 trillion,â€‌ said Lori Grooms, director of Infection Prevention and Control at [OSF HealthCare](https://www.osfhealthcare.org/). â€œWhen a bacteria or virus invades the body, the intruder begins to multiply and attack or mimic our cells, infecting us and causing us to become ill.â€‌

Our immune system has several ways to fight the intruders. While our blood carries oxygen to tissues and organs, it also contains [white blood cells](http://healthlibrary.osfhealthcare.org/Search/167,white_cell_count), or immune cells, that fight infection.

## Antibodies to the rescue

![How vaccines work infographic](https://osf-blog.live.imagescape.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/Vaccines_Infographic-240x300.jpg)

[Download](https://osf-blog.live.imagescape.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/How-Vaccines-Work.pdf)

Antibodies are some of the first cells to respond to the alarm. These antibodies attach to the intruding pathogen (virus or bacteria). These antibodies then attract other immune cells to help in fighting off infections. These immune cells consist primarily of macrophages, B-lymphocytes and T-lymphocytes:

When our immune system recognizes an invader, it starts to mobilize its forces to deal with the threat. The mobilization can take several days.

â€œAfter we defeat the infection, our bodies often catalog the invader and remember how to protect us against that disease in the future,â€‌ Grooms said. â€œItâ€™s a constant battle worthy of Hollywoodâ€™s biggest screen.â€‌

Some antibodies provide immunity for a lifetime, [while others do not last as long](http://healthlibrary.osfhealthcare.org/Search/85,P01463) â€“ but your body has already learned how to fight off the virus.

## How vaccinations help our bodies fight infection

[Vaccinations](https://www.osfhealthcare.org/blog/vaccinations-they-are-for-adults-too/)آ work by imitating a bacteria or virus. While vaccines traditionally use a dead or weakened version of the virus, thereâ€™s also a new type of vaccine that uses messenger RNA (mRNA). This new way ofآ [developing a vaccine](https://www.osfhealthcare.org/blog/the-history-of-vaccines-and-how-theyre-developed/)آ was used to create COVID-19 vaccines. COVID-19 mRNA vaccines give instructions for our cells to make a harmless piece of what is called the â€œspike proteinâ€‌ of the coronavirus. The spike protein is found on the surface of the virus that causes COVID-19.

Once the instructions are inside the immune cells, the cells use them to make the protein piece. After the protein piece is made, the cell breaks down the instructions and gets rid of them.

Next, the cell displays the protein piece on its surface. Our immune systems recognize that the protein doesnâ€™t belong there and begin building an immune response and making antibodies, like what happens in natural infection against COVID-19.

As is the case in traditional vaccines, our immune system pre-catalogs the invader and is more prepared to attack it should we become infected for real. The imitation infection caused by a vaccine may causeآ [minor symptoms](https://www.osfhealthcare.org/blog/vaccine-side-effects-what-you-should-know/), such as fever. You should expect these minor symptoms as your body builds immunity.

â€œOnce your body makes the required antibodies, your body remembers how to fight that disease in the future,â€‌ Grooms said. â€œRemember, because it takes a couple of days to weeks for our bodies to build up an immunity, itâ€™s still possible to get infected by the targeted disease just before or just after the vaccination.â€‌

## Boosters

When it comes to getting vaccinated to protect yourself against a disease, booster shots can be a normal part of the process.

â€œBoosterâ€‌ refers to an extra dose of a vaccine given after the original (or primary) dose is administered. These boosters can be given weeks, months or years later.

Some vaccines are administered using the strategy of giving the original (or primary) dose along with a booster dose. With the original dose, the immune system is primed to recognize and produce antibodies against the virus it was created to defend.

Booster shots are administered to remind the bodyâ€™s immune system about the virus it needs to defend against. This improves or gives the immune system a boost.

## Stay up-to-date on vaccinations

Vaccinations are a [safe](https://www.osfhealthcare.org/blog/how-to-ensure-that-vaccines-are-safe/) way to build up your bodyâ€™s immunity to certain diseases, helping it prepare to fight the disease in the future.

Itâ€™s important to stay current on required and [recommended immunizations](https://healthlibrary.osfhealthcare.org/Search/SearchResults.pg?SearchPhrase=Immunization%20Recommendations&SearchType=text&SearchOperator=And) for you and your family. Talk to your [primary care provider](https://providers.osfhealthcare.org/search?primary_care=Primary%20Care%20Providers&sort=networks%2C%20relevance) about what vaccines you may need.

#### Explore more:

#### About the author David Pruitt

David Pruitt is a writer for the Marketing & Communications division of OSF HealthCare. He has a bachelorâ€™s of journalism from Southern Illinois University Edwardsville and worked as a reporter before joining OSF HealthCare in 2014.
  
  
An avid golfer and fisherman, David was born and raised Alton, Illinois, which is where he currently resides with his son, James.

--- SOURCE 5: 10 Reasons to Get Vaccinated ---
URL: https://www.nfid.org/immunization/10-reasons-to-get-vaccinated/

SUMMARY:
Vaccine-preventable diseases have not gone away آ· Vaccines help keep you healthy آ· Vaccines are as important to your overall health as diet and exercise

FULL CONTENT:
![NFID](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/NFID-Main-Logo-Standard.png)

# 10 Reasons to Get Vaccinated

1. [Home](https://www.nfid.org)
2. [Immunization](https://www.nfid.org/immunization/)
3. 10 Reasons to Get Vaccinated

## 10 Reasons to Get Vaccinated

![Reason 1: Vaccine-preventable diseases have not gone away](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-1-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 2: Vaccines help keep you healthy](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-2-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 3: Vaccines are as important to your overall health as diet and exercise](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-3B-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 4: Vaccination can mean the difference between life and death](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-4-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 5: Vaccines are safe](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 5: Vaccines are safe](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-5-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 6: Vaccines can't give you the diseases they are designed to prevent](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 6: Vaccines can't give you the diseases they are designed to prevent](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-6-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 7: Young and healthy people can get very sick too](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 7: Young and healthy people can get very sick too](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-7-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 8: Vaccine-preventable diseases are expensive](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 8: Vaccine-preventable diseases are expensive](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-8-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 9: When you get sick, your children, grandchildren, and parents are at risk too](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 9: When you get sick, your children, grandchildren, and parents are at risk too](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-9-1920x960-1.jpg)
![Reason 10: Everyone deserves the chance to stay healthy](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='1920'%20height='960'%20viewBox='0%200%201920%20960'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Reason 10: Everyone deserves the chance to stay healthy](https://www.nfid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Reason-10-1920x960-1.jpg)

Talk with a trusted healthcare professional about [**vaccines recommended for you and your family**](https://www.nfid.org/immunization/us-vaccine-recommendations/)

Updated July 2025

Source: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

Founded in 1973, the National Foundation for Infectious Diseases (NFID) is an independent non-profit 501(c)(3) organization dedicated to educating and engaging the public, communities, and healthcare professionals about infectious diseases across the lifespan.

This website is designed to provide education to the public and healthcare professionals about infectious diseases across the lifespan. All information on this site is for general purposes only, is based on US recommendations, and is not intended to be a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Contact a qualified healthcare professional if you have questions or concerns about your health.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Vaccination effectively prevents infectious diseases by stimulating the immune system, enabling the body to recognize and combat pathogens without causing illness.
Vaccination is vital for public health, reducing the incidence of diseases, preventing outbreaks, and safeguarding vulnerable populations who cannot be vaccinated.
Vaccines significantly improve global health outcomes, reducing mortality rates from infectious diseases and contributing to increased life expectancy worldwide.
Widespread vaccination reduces the likelihood of disease outbreaks, as fewer individuals can transmit infections, protecting entire communities.
Vaccination builds herd immunity, protecting those unable to receive vaccines, such as infants or immunocompromised individuals, by decreasing overall disease transmission.
